description:
Weight problem testing

BASIC 


  • Thyrhoid hormones – TSH, Free T4, FreeT3 etc
  • Sex Hormones – Testosterone, Estradiol etc.
  • Stress hormones - Cortisol
  • CRP
  • Complete Blood Count( CBC)
  • Lipid Panel- Cholesterol etc  
  • Complete Metabolic Panel – Ca, Uria, Creatinine, Na, K, etc 


EXPERT 


  • Vit D 25 hydroxy
  • Insulin
  • Ferritin
  • HbA1c


RECOMMENDED FOR: 


  • Weight problems 


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


  • Weight loss 
  • Weight gain
  • Weakness
  • dizziness


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • take your medications as prescribed. 
  • If you are supplementing with any hormones, take them approximately 2 hours prior to having your blood drawn to see peak levels. 
  • Hormones are best drawn between 8-10 AM. 
  • Hormonal contraceptives can interfere with progesterone and estrogen results. 

RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


Cortisol level higher- stress


LOWER VALUE MEANING 


Estrogen level lower – anorexia, bulimia, menopause 


TIP

  • Pre-menopausal women: For a 28 day cycle, blood should be drawn on day 21 (day 1 is the day that bleeding begins). For longer or shorter cycles, count 8 days back from the day your next menstruation would start.
  • Post-menopausal women: Blood can be drawn any day of the month

GENETIC TESTS

WHAT ARE THEY 

Genetic tests examine a person's genes, usually in order to estimate their risk of a particular disease, determine the exact cause of cell changes, or detect any hereditary diseases

WHAT THEY CAN TELL ME 

  • Down syndrome
  • Huntington disease
  • Cystic fibrosis
  • Colon cancer
  • Breast cancer
  • Sickle cell anemai 
  • Phenylketonuria 
  • Prenatal testing
  • Paternity
  • Crime suspect

HOW THEY ARE DONE 

  • With a blood sample 
  • From a small amount of saliva


RISKS 

  • Can cause anxiety knowing that you have risk for a serious disease 
  • Results are not 100% accurate

TIP

  • genetic testing cannot detect every disease 
  • a positive result doesn’t necessarily mean you’ll develop a disease

Positron-emission tomography (PET)

WHAT IS A PET SCAN?

Positron emission tomography is a functional imaging technique that uses radioactive substances known as radiotracers to visualize and measure changes in metabolic processes, and in other physiological activities including blood flow, regional chemical composition, and absorption


RECCOMENDED FOR 

  • Cancer
  • Brain disease
  • Heart disease
  • Response to cancer treatment 
  • HOW TO PREPARE FOR A PET SCAN 
  • Avoid exercising 24 hours before the scan.
  • Follow a special diet for 12 to 24 hours before the scan.
  • Don't drink or eat anything, except water, for 6 hours before the scan


RISKS 

  • Pregnancy, breastfeeding 
  • the contrast material can cause medical problems or allergic reactions.
  • most reactions are mild and result in a rash or itchiness. In rare instances, an allergic reaction can be serious, even life-threatening
  • Claustrophobia 
  • if you have metal inside your body, you may not be able to have an MRI
  • Cochlear implants
  • A pacemaker
  • Metal plates, screws, or rods
  • Artificial heart valves
  • An intrauterine device (IUD)
  • A drug pump implant
  • Artificial joints
  • Dental fillings, bridges or other metal dental work
  • Worked in the metal working industry (and could have metal dust in your eyes)

AFTER PET SCAN 

  • After the exam you can return to your normal routine. 
  • If you were given contrast material, drink lots of fluids to help your kidneys remove the contrast material from your body
  • In some cases, you may be asked to wait for a short time before leaving to ensure that you feel well after the exam


RESULTS

  • PET scan images are stored as electronic data files and are usually reviewed on a computer screen. A radiologist interprets these images and sends a report to your doctor


TIP

  • If you are claustrophobic before the test you can use a natural relaxation medication or even an anxiolytic

INFECTIOUS DISEASES TESTS

BASIC 

  • CBC
  • CRP
  • ESR
  • LIVER TESTS
  • KIDNEY TESTS

EXPERT 

  • Antibodies Borelia

  • Zika

RECOMMENDED FOR:

I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 

  • Fever
  • Diarrhea
  • Fatigue
  • Muscle aches
  • Coughing
  • Been bitten by animal 


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

Ig G antibodies CMV ( Cytomegalovirus) increased - past infection to CMC (fatigue, muscle aches) 

LOWER VALUE MEANING

DEXA SCAN

WHAT IS A DEXA SCAN?

DEXA SCAN or Bone density scan is a type of X-ray that measures bone mineral density (BMD). Two X-ray beams, with different energy levels, are aimed at the patient's bones. When soft tissue absorption is subtracted out, the bone mineral density can be determined from the absorption of each beam by bone.

It is a quick and painless procedure that involves lying on your back

RECCOMENDED FOR 

  • Osteoporosis
  • Osteopenia

HOW TO PREPARE FOR A DEXA SCAN 

  • stop taking any calcium supplements for 24 hours before the test
  • remove any clothes that have metal fasteners, such as zips, hooks or buckles

RISKS 

  • The amount of radiation used in DEXA scans is very low and similar to the amount of radiation used in common X-rays

AFTER DEXA SCAN 

  • You can go home and do things as usual

RESULTS

A bone density scan compares your bone density with the bone density expected for a young healthy adult or a healthy adult of your own age, gender and ethnicity.

The difference is calculated as a standard deviation (SD) score. This measures the difference between your bone density and the expected value.

The difference between your measurement and that of a young healthy adult is known as a T score

  • above -1 SD is normal
  • between -1 and -2.5 SD is defined as mildly reduced bone mineral density (BMD) compared with peak bone mass (PBM)
  • at or below -2.5 SD is defined as osteoporosis


TIP

Wear comfortable clothing

HORMONES WOMAN

BASIC 

  • Estrogen
  • Progesterone
  • Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)


EXPERT 

  • Testosterone/DHEA
  • Thyroid hormones


RECOMMENDED FOR: 

  • screening of a women’s hormone status
  • effectiveness of hormone replacement therapies
  • PCOS 
  • ovarian cysts
  • cancer ovarian 
  • thyroid disease

I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 

  • menstrual issues
  • fatigue
  • loss of sex drive
  • problems getting pregnant
  • changes in sex characteristics


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?

  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • take your medications as prescribed. 
  • If you are supplementing with any hormones, take them approximately 2 hours prior to having your blood drawn to see peak levels. 
  • Hormones are best drawn between 8-10 AM.


RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children

HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

Estrogen High - Light or heavy menstrual bleeding, Worsened premenstrual syndrome, Loss of sex drive



LOWER VALUE MEANING 

Low FSH low-  

Being very underweight or having had recent rapid weight loss, Not producing eggs (not ovulating)

Parts of the brain (the pituitary gland or hypothalamus) not producing normal amounts of some or all of its hormones, Pregnancy

TIP

Certain medications are also known to increase estrogen levels. These include steroid hormones, antibiotics 

progesterone tests to check ovulation are typically done on day 21 of your cycle

Cortisol test

PHOTO DOCTOR   NAME AND SPECIALTY, WHERE HE WORKS - LINK TO FULL BIO. 

BASIC 

  • Cortisol 

EXPERT 

  • Neurotransmitters Tests

RECOMMENDED FOR: 

  • Anxiety
  • Cushing Syndrome
  • Adison Disease 
  • I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 
  • Stress for long time 
  • Weight gain
  • Increased fat around the base of the neck or between the shoulder blades
  • Easy bruising
  • Wide purple streaks on the stomach, breasts, hips, and under the arms
  • Muscle weakness
  • Long-lasting fatigue
  • Loss of appetite
  • Weight loss
  • Abdominal (belly) pain
  • Nausea, vomiting 

WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?

  • rest for a bit before your sample is taken
  • avoid eating, drinking, or brushing your teeth for a certain time period before your test


RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children

HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


LOWER VALUE MEANING 

TIP

BIOPSY

WHAT IS BIOPSY?

A biopsy is a process that involves extraction of sample cells or tissues for examination to determine the presence or extent of a disease medical. Is performed by a surgeon, interventional radiologist, or an interventional cardiologist 

BENEFITS 

Determine if you have

  • cancer 
  • autoimmune disease
  • infection
  • inflammation 

RISKS 

  • Infection
  • Bleeding 

WHAT TO DO BEFORE A BIOPSY 

  • IT may require some preparation such as bowel prep, clear liquid diet, or nothing by mouth
  • Always ask your doctor for further information on any recommended medical procedure.

TIP

  • Non-surgical procedures are used to diagnose, measure or treat problems such as disease or injury.
  • All medical procedures have benefits, risks and possible side effects.
  •  You may need to stop taking certain drugs( aspirin, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications) before a biopsy

Inflammation 

BASIC 


  • Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (sed rate or ESR
  • CRP 
  • Ferritin 


EXPERT 


  • Procalcitonin
  • Fibrinogen 


RECOMMENDED FOR: 


  • Inflammation
  • Infection
  • Autoimmune diseases – arteritis, polymyalgia 


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


  • Recurrent infections 
  • Inflammation 
  • Muscle stiffness 
  • Fever and sweating 
  • Head pain ( temple area)
  • Vision loss
  • Weight loss


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • avoid drinking alcohol 
  • avoid taking medications - Statins, Acetaminophen, NSAID


RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children

HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

Fibrinogen higher – inflammation of artery 

PCT higher - bacterial infection 

LOWER VALUE MEANING 


TIP

  • CRP higher shows increased cardiovascular risk

GLUCOSE TEST

BASIC 

Fasting Blood Sugar( Glucose) Test 

EXPERT 


  • A1C Test. The A1C test measures your average blood sugar level over the past 2 or 3 months
  • Glucose Tolerance Test
  • Glucose Tolerance Test
  • C Peptide
  • Insulin sensitivity HOMA INDEX 


RECOMMENDED FOR: 


  • Diabetes type 1 or 2 


  • Screening for diabetes 


SYMPTOMS OF GLUCOSE PROBLEM


  • extreme thirst
  • feeling tired all the time
  • feeling very hungry, even after eating
  • blurry vision
  • frequent urination
  • sores or cuts that won’t heal
  • dizziness 
  • sweating
  • heart palpitations 
  • shakiness
  • anxiety



WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • avoid drinking alcohol 


RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children.



HIGHER GLUCOSE VALUE MEANING 


  • Prediabetes 


  • Diabetes type 1 or 2 


  • hyperthyroidism, or overactive thyroid
  • pancreatitis, or inflammation of your pancreas
  • pancreatic cancer
  • stress to the body from illness, trauma, or surgery
  • medications like steroids
  • Cushing syndrome, in which the body produces too much cortisol
  • acromegaly, when there is too much growth hormone



LOWER VALUE MEANING 


  • Blood Glucose is lower if:


  • Medication ( oral diabetic medication, quinine) 


  • Excessive alcohol drinking 


  • Severe infection 


  • Malnutrition


  • Tumor pancreas , adrenal , pituitary 


TIP


  • Be sure to talk to your provider about what you are currently taking, including over-the-counter medicines, vitamins and supplements
  • coffee can interfere with blood test results because it contains caffeine and soluble plant matter, is also a diuretic, which means that it will increase how much you pee
  • If you have questions or concerns about test preparations always talk to your health care provider

Minerals 

BASIC 


  • Na 
  • K 
  • Mg 
  • Ca
  • Phos
  • Cl


EXPERT 


  • Iron
  • Iod
  • Selenium
  • Zincum



RECOMMENDED FOR: 


  • Diarrhoea
  • Vomiting 
  • Drug abuse
  • Burns
  • Diabetes
  • Cancer
  • Kidney problem
  • Liver problem 
  • Heart problem




I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


  • frequent urination and increased thirst
  • Fatigue
  • Headaches.
  • Nausea and vomiting.
  • Loss of appetite.
  • Constipation and abdominal pain.
  • Muscle cramps
  • Dry, scaly skin
  • Brittle nails
  • Confusion
  • Irritability or restlessness
  • Depression
  • Abnormal heart beats 



WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


No special care needed 


RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children

HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

Magnesium higher – arrythmia, cardiac arrest 

LOWER VALUE MEANING 

Natrium lower – nausea, vomiting, seizures, coma

I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE:

  • Abdominal pain

  • Constipation 
  • Nausea vomiting 

WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?

  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • avoid drinking alcohol

RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children

HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

Amylase higher – acute pancreatitis, blockacke duct in pancreas, pancreatic cancer 

LOWER VALUE MEANING 

Lipase lower- chronic pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis

Blood clothing and circulation 

BASIC 


  • PT PTT INR
  • Thrombocites 
  • Ddimer
  • Fibrinogen


EXPERT 


  • Viscosity seroum 
  • Factor VIII Activity Blood Test
  • C-Reactive protein


RECOMMENDED FOR: 


  • Blood clothing and circulation 


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


  • sudden breathlessness
  • sharp chest pain (may be worse when you breathe in) 
  • cough or coughing up blood
  • Swollen, painful vein
  • Bleeding 
  • pain, swelling, redness and warmth in a leg or arm


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


  • medications (Aspirin, Clopidogrel etc) can affect tests so may need to be discontinued  
  • talk with your doctor always before testing 


RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


D Dimer increased – blood clot, pregnancy, heart disease 


LOWER VALUE MEANING 


Fibrinogen lower- danger of bleeding

Cardiovascular testing

BASIC 

  • Cholesterol (total)
  • LDL cholesterol
  • HDL cholesterol
  • VLDL cholesterol
  • Triglycerides
  • CPK
  • LDH
  • Troponin
  • Hemogram
  • Glucose 
  • CRP 

EXPERT 

  • Homocysteine
  • Omega 3
  • coQ10
  • PLAC
  • B2 type natriuretic peptide 

RECOMMENDED FOR: 

  • Cardiovascular problems

I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 

  • Chest pain
  • pain, weakness or numb legs and/or arms
  • Breathing problem
  • Palpitations
  • Weakness
  • Dizziness 
  • Swollen limbs

WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?

  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • avoid drinking alcohol 
  • avoid taking medications - Statins, Acetaminophen, NSAID


RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

Troponin higher – heart attack, myocarditis 

CPK higher – injury muscle, heart or brain 

Cholesterol higher – higher risk of heart problems and stroke 


LOWER VALUE MEANING 

CoQ10 lower – heart problems or taking medication( statins)

CHOLESTEROL TEST (LIPID PANEL)

BASIC 


  • Cholesterol
  • Triglycerides
  • LDL
  • HDL


EXPERT 


  • Omega 3, Omega 6 
  • Lipoprotein a 
  • Very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) 


RECOMMENDED FOR: 


  1. Screening cardiovascular test
  2. Monitor response to cholesterol medication
  3. Liver disease 


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


  • yellowish, fatty bumps or yellow creases on the skin, formed by an accumulation of fatty deposits around tendons and joints (xanthomas)
  • white arcs around the cornea of the eye (arcus senilis)
  • heart problem
  • fatty liver 
  • diabetes
  • heart problem
  • stroke 


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • avoid drinking alcohol 
  • avoid taking medications - Statins, Acetaminophen, NSAID


RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


Cholesterol high – higher risk for heart problems and stroke 


LOWER VALUE MEANING

  • epinephrine
  • glutamate 
  • GABA

  • Phenylalanine

I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE:

  • Low mood 
  • Mood swings
  • Anger 
  • Increased appetite 
  • Sleep problem
  • Migraines 
  • Dependence on caffeine 
  • Sexual dysfunction

WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?

  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • take your medications as prescribed. 
  • If you are supplementing with any hormones, take them approximately 2 hours prior to having your blood drawn to see peak levels. 
  • Hormones are best drawn between 8-10 AM. 


RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


Epinephrine higher – stress 


LOWER VALUE MEANING 


Lower serotonin – depression 


TIP

  • 5-HIAA Test.- avoid avocados, bananas, pineapples, walnuts, and eggplants

Food intolerance tests 

BASIC 


Antibodies – IGE, IGG


EXPERT 


Immune Tests 


RECOMMENDED FOR: 


Food intolerances 

Check response on treatment for food intolerances 


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


Diarrhea

Bloating

Abdominal pain 

Constipation 


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


No special care needed


RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


Higher IGE anibodies to a specific food means that there is an allergy or intolerance to that food


LOWER VALUE MEANING 


TIP

If the value is low it is not needed to remove the food from diet, except if the person has bad symptoms when ingesting it

XRAY

WHAT IS AN XRAY?

An Xray or Radiography is a photographic or digital image of a part of the body produced by X-rays. As they pass the body, they get absorbed to different degrees by different tissues and this is used to generate an image.

RECCOMENDED FOR 

  • Bone fractures
  • Tumors
  • Pneumonia
  • Arthritis
  • Dislocated joint
  • Foreign objects
  • Swelling 
  • Heart failure
  • check the position of wires, leads and tubes after surgical procedures

HOW TO PREPARE FOR AN XRAY

  • Remove metal objects like jewelry, hairpins or hearing aids.
  • Stop eating or drinking several hours beforehand (for GI X-rays).
  • Wear comfortable clothing or change into a gown before the X-ray.


RISKS 

  • X-rays should be avoided in pregnancy

AFTER XRAY

  • After the exam you can return to your normal routine. 
  • If you were given contrast material, drink lots of fluids to help your kidneys remove the contrast material from your body


  • In some cases, you may be asked to wait for a short time before leaving to ensure that you feel well after the exam.


RESULTS

  • Xray images are stored as electronic data files and are usually reviewed on a computer screen. A radiologist interprets these images and sends a report to your doctor


TIP

  • radiation dose absorbed from a chest X ray is quite low and is smaller than that received annually from natural sources


  • Avoid using lotions, creams or perfume

Heavy metals test

BASIC 


  • Arsenic
  • Cadmium
  • Chromium
  • Lead
  • Mercury


EXPERT 


  • Aluminium
  • Copper
  • Iron
  • Manganese
  • Nickel
  • Selenium
  • Silicon
  • Thallium
  • Zinc



RECOMMENDED FOR: 


  • Heavy metals excess 


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


  • Abdominal pain
  • Chills
  • Diarrhea
  • Muscle weakness
  • Nausea or vomiting
  • Shortness of breath (dyspnea)
  • Tingling in your hands or feet


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


  • No special care needed 


RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


High levels could point to heavy metal poisoning


LOWER VALUE MEANING 


TIP

  • Chelation therapy involves taking medicines by mouth or injection to remove excess metals

MAMMOGRAPHY

WHAT IS A MAMMOGRAPHY?

A mammogram is an X-ray picture of the breast

RECCOMENDED FOR 

  • Detection of breast cancer


HOW TO PREPARE FOR A MAMMOGRAPHY

  • Try to avoid the week just before your period( menstrual cycle). 
  • On the day of the exam, don't apply deodorant, antiperspirant, powders, lotions, creams, or perfumes under your arms, or on or under your breasts. Some of these contain substances that can show up on the x-ray as white spots


RISKS 

  • overdiagnosis
  • false-positives results
  • anxiety
  • radiation injury if they are done many times 

AFTER MAMMOGRAPHY

HEMOGRAM OR COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT (CBC)

BASIC 


  • White blood cell count (WBC)
  • Red blood cell count (RBC)
  • Platelet count.
  • Hematocrit red blood cell volume (HCT)
  • Hemoglobin concentration (HB). This is the oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells.
  • Differential white blood count.
  • Red blood cell indices (measurements)



EXPERT 


  • Reticulocyte Count 
  • Prothrombin time (PT) Partial Thromboplastin Time (PTT) 
  • International Normalized Ratio (INR) 


RECOMMENDED FOR: 


  • Anemia
  • Infections
  • Cancers
  • Weight loss
  • Pains 


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


  • Fever
  • Coughing 
  • weakness
  • fatigue or loss of energy
  • pain
  • weight loss
  • jaundice (yellow skin and eyes)
  • discoloured bodily discharge (dark urine or light stools)
  • nausea
  • vomiting
  • diarrhea
  • atypical bruising or bleeding



WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


  • you can eat and drink as usual before the test


RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


Increased Leucocytes – infection, inflammation, leukemia, reaction to medications


Increased Platelets – infection, inflammation, cancer, trauma, iron deficiency


LOWER VALUE MEANING 


Decreased Leucocytes – leukemia, lymphoma, aplastic anemia, viral infection, chemotherapy, autoimmune disease 


Decreased Platelets – blood clots , thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), medications

STOOL TESTS 

A stool test examines a sample of faeces (poo) in the laboratory. Microbial analysis (culturing), microscopy and chemical tests are among the tests performed on stool samples


BASIC 

  • Fecal occult blood tests
  • White blood cells 
  • Ph test 
  • Rotavirus test
  • Yersinia test 
  • Salmonella Culture test 

EXPERT 

  • Parasites
  • Toxins - Clostridium dificile 
  • Giardia antigen test 
  • Fat 
  • Calprotectin Test


RECOMMENDED FOR: 

  • Gastrointestinal infections
  • Crohn’s disease
  • ulcerative colitis 
  • Bowel cancer
  • Polyps


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 

  • Diarrhoea
  • Vomiting
  • Abdominal pain
  • Blood in stool
  • Haemorrhoids 
  • Weight loss 
  • Anorexia 
  • Change in bowel habit 

WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?

  • collect your poo (stool) sample in a completely clean (sterile) container
  • store the container in a fridge in a sealed plastic bag if you can't hand it in straight away



RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children

HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

Detection of blood in stool - bowel cancer, polyps, haemorrhoids 

LOWER VALUE MEANING 

PH stool low - poor absorption of carbohydrate or fat 

TIP 

  • wash your hands thoroughly with soap and warm running water
  • to find blood in stool it usually requires stool samples from two or three separate bowel movements

Rheumatological

BASIC 


  • CRP C-reactive protein
  • ESR erythrocyte sedimentation rate
  • RF Rheumatoid Factor
  • Ca
  • Vit D
  • CPK
  • Uric acid 
  • Kidney tests
  • CBC



EXPERT 


  • Anti-CCP ( Anticycylic citrullinated peptide)
  • ANA ( Antinuclear antbodies)
  • Cyclic Citrullinated Peptide (CCP): a more specific test for rheumatoid arthritis
  • Complement (e.g. C3, C4)
  • Serum Protein Electrophoresis (SPEP)
  • HLA-B27
  • Anticardiolipin Antibodies (ACL), lupus anticoagulant (LAC), Beta-2-Glycoprotein-1 (B2GP1
  • Angiotensin Converting Enzyme (ACE)



RECOMMENDED FOR: 


  • Rheumatoid arthritis 
  • Osteoarthritis
  • Ankylosing spondylitis
  • Lupus
  • Vasculitis


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


  • Joint pain.
  • Swelling in a joint or joints.
  • Joint stiffness that lasts for at least 1 hour in the early morning.
  • Chronic pain or tenderness in a joint or joints.
  • Warmth and redness in the joint area.
  • Limited movement in the affected joint or joints
  • Fever 
  • Weight loss


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?



RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children


HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


RF Rheumatoid Factor higher – rheumathoid arthritis, cancer, chronic infections, smoking  



LOWER VALUE MEANING 



TIP

  • Anti-Neutrophil Cytoplasmic Antibody (ANCA): antibodies that can be seen in rare rheumatic diseases such as vasculitis

ENDOSCOPY

WHAT IS ENDOSCOPY?

An endoscopy is a medical technique where a slender tube (endoscope) is inserted into the body, which allows the doctor to make a visual inspection.

Examples of endoscopic procedures include colonoscopy (bowel), gastroscopy (stomach), cystoscopy (bladder), bronchoscopy (airways of the lung) and laparoscopy (abdomen).

BENEFITS 

GASTROSCOPY

  • Gastric and duodenal ulcer
  • Gastroesophageal reflux
  • Cancer stomach 
  • Biopsy 

COLONOSCOPY

  • Colon cancer
  • Crohns disease
  • Ulcerative colitis 
  • Diverticulosis
  • Bleeding 
  • Intestinal blockage 

CYSTOSCOPY 

  • Blader Cancer 
  • Urinary tract Infection 
  • Narrowing 
  • Bleeding 
  • Blader Stones 
  • Biopsy 

BRONCHOSCOPY

  • Tumors or bronchial cancer
  • Airway blockage (obstruction)
  • Narrowed areas in airways (strictures)
  • Inflammation and infections (tuberculosis, pneumonia)
  • Interstitial pulmonary disease
  • Causes of persistent cough
  • Causes of coughing up blood
  • Spots seen on chest X-rays

LAPAROSCOPY 

  • Endometriosis
  • Infertility 
  • Chronic pelvic pain
  • Removal fibroids, uterus, ovarian cysts
  • Uterine Prolapse 
  • Urinary incontinence 
  • Cancers 


RISKS

GASTROSCOPPY – COLONOSCOPY 

  • Bleeding. This is a rare complication following upper endoscopy and biopsy
  • Perforation
  • Reaction to Anaesthetic
  • Other Injury

CYSTOSCOPY

  • Infection. Rarely, cystoscopy can introduce germs into your urinary tract, causing an infection
  • Bleeding. Cystoscopy might cause some blood in your urine
  • Pain. After the procedure, you might experience abdominal pain and a burning sensation when you urinate


BRONCHOSCOPY

  • Bleeding.
  • Infection.
  • Hole in the airway (bronchial perforation)
  • Irritation of the airways (bronchospasm)
  • Irritation of the vocal cords (laryngospasm)
  • Air in the space between the lung covering (pleural space) that causes the lung to collapse (pneumothorax)


LAPAROSCOPY 

  • bleeding and the potential need for a blood transfusion.
  • infection.
  • hernia.
  • a risk of damage to internal structures, such as such as blood vessels, the stomach, bowel, bladder, or ureter.
  • adverse reactions to anesthesia.
  • abdominal inflammation or infection.
  • blood clots.


TIPS 

  • Non-surgical procedures are used to diagnose, measure or treat problems such as disease or injury.
  • All medical procedures have benefits, risks and possible side effects.
  • Always ask your doctor for further information on any recommended medical procedure.

ANEMIA TEST 

BASIC 


  • Hemogram or Complete blood count (CBC)
  • Reticulocyte count
  • Iron profile
  • Peripheral blood smear
  • Kidney function tests 
  • Thyroid function tests
  • Coagulation screen
  • Liver function tests (LFT)
  • fecal occult blood test


EXPERT 


  • Hemolysis profile
  • Hemoglobin electrophoresis
  • Bone marrow analysis
  • Vitamin screen tests


RECOMMENDED FOR: 


Anemia of different causes like


  • Iron deficiency.
  • Vitamin B12 deficiency.
  • Folate deficiency
  • Certain medications ( cephalosporin antibiotics)
  • Destruction of red blood cells earlier than normal (which may be caused by immune system problems)
  • chronic diseases – kidney ulcerative colitis
  • cancer
  • rheumathoid arthritis



I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 


  • weakness
  • fatigue or loss of energy
  • pain
  • weight loss
  • jaundice (yellow skin and eyes)
  • discolored bodily discharge (dark urine or light stools)
  • atypical bruising or bleeding


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?


  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • avoid drinking alcohol 
  • avoid taking medications - Statins, Acetaminophen, NSAID
  • Avoiding certain supplements -aloe vera, black cohosh, cascara, chaparral, comfrey, ephedra, or kava



RESULTS 


Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children



HIGHER VALUE MEANING 


Increased Leucocytes – infection, inflammation, leukemia, reaction to medications


Increased Platelets – infection, inflammation, cancer, trauma, iron deficiency


Reticulocyte count higher – bleeding, hemolytic anemia 


LOWER VALUE MEANING 


Decreased Leucocytes – leukemia, lymphoma, aplastic anemia, viral infection, chemotherapy, autoimmune disease 


Decreased Platelets – blood clots , thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), medications 


INR lower – risk for blood clots 


TIP

  • Red meats, broccoli, and even some medications may cause a false positive fecal occult blood test

MRI (magnetic resonance imaging)

WHAT IS AN MRI SCAN?

Magnetic resonance imaging is a medical imaging technique used in radiology to form pictures of the anatomy and the physiological processes of the body. MRI scanners use strong magnetic fields, magnetic field gradients, and radio waves to generate images of the organs in the body

RECCOMENDED FOR 

  • Soft tissue injuries
  • Cancers
  • Brain problems 
  • Spinal cord problems
  • Aneurysms
  • Heart problems
  • Lung problems 
  • Sports injuries 
  • Bone diseases and conditions.
  • Pelvic problems (in women) or prostate problems (in men)


HOW TO PREPARE FOR A MRI SCAN 

  • Refrain from eating or drinking for a few hours before your scan
  • Tell your doctor if you had any allergic reactions
  • Take off some or all of your clothing and wear a hospital gown
  • Remove metal objects, such as a belt, jewelry, dentures and eyeglasses, which might interfere with image results


RISKS 

  • Pregnancy, breastfeeding 
  • the contrast material can cause medical problems or allergic reactions.
  • most reactions are mild and result in a rash or itchiness. In rare instances, an allergic reaction can be serious, even life-threatening. 
  • Claustrophobia 
  • if you have metal inside your body, you may not be able to have an MRI
  • Cochlear implants.
  • A pacemaker.
  • Metal plates, screws, or rods.
  • Artificial heart valves.
  • An intrauterine device (IUD).
  • A drug pump implant.
  • Artificial joints.
  • Dental fillings, bridges or other metal dental work.
  • Worked in the metal working industry (and could have metal dust in your eyes).


AFTER MRI SCAN 

  • After the exam you can return to your normal routine. 
  • If you were given contrast material, drink lots of fluids to help your kidneys remove the contrast material from your body
  • In some cases, you may be asked to wait for a short time before leaving to ensure that you feel well after the exam


RESULTS

  • MRI images are stored as electronic data files and are usually reviewed on a computer screen. A radiologist interprets these images and sends a report to your doctor


TIP

  • If you are claustrophobic before the test you can use a natural relaxation medication or even an anxiolytic

SALIVA TESTS

Saliva testing is a diagnostic technique that involves laboratory analysis of saliva to identify markers of endocrine, immunologic, inflammatory, infectious, and other types of conditions. 

BASIC 

  • Alcohol detection
  • Cortisol 
  • Covid 
  • Cocaine
  • Marihuana
  • Benzodiazepines 

EXPERT 

  • Cancer
  • DNA
  • RNA


RECOMMENDED FOR: 

  • Alcohol detection
  • Drug detection
  • Amphetamines
  • Barbiturates
  • Benzodiazepines
  • Cocaine
  • Marijuana
  • Methamphetamine
  • Opioids
  • Covid 
  • Cushing disease
  • Cancer
  • DNA, RNA


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 

  • Flu symptoms
  • Allergy 
  • Anxiety 
  • Hypertension 
  • Low libido 
  • Confusion
  • Euphoria 


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?

  • Brush your teeth and/or use mouthwash. 
  • Don't eat, drink, smoke, or chew gum or tobacco for 30 minutes after brushing your teeth and before providing your saliva sample


RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children

HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

Amphetamines higher - amphetamine dose 

LOWER VALUE MEANING 

Cortisol lower - Addison disease, secondary adrenal insufficiency, autoimmune diseases

ULTRASOUND 

WHAT IS AN ULTRASOUND?

Ultrasound is a noninvasive imaging test that shows structures inside your body using high-intensity sound waves. 

RECCOMENDED FOR 

  • Pains in different parts of body ( abdomen – kidney stones)
  • Swelling
  • Infection 
  • Cysts ( breast, thyroid) 
  • Obstructions 
  • Heart problems 


HOW TO PREPARE FOR AN ULTRASOUND 

  • drink water and not go to the toilet until after the scan – this may be needed before a scan of your unborn baby or your pelvic area
  • avoid eating or drinking for several hours before the scan – this may be needed before a scan of your digestive system, including the liver and gallbladder


RISKS 

  • Because ultrasound uses sound waves instead of radiation, it's safer than X-rays
  • It can be done during pregnancy and breastfeeding 

AFTER ULTRASOUND 

  • In most cases, there are no after-effects and you can go home soon after the scan is finished.


RESULTS


TIP

Wear comfortable clothing

LIVER FUNCTION TESTS (LVT)

BASIC 

ALT (alanine transaminase), 

AST (aspartate aminotransferase)

Gamma-glutamyl tansferase (GGT).


EXPERT 

  • Alkaline phosphatase (ALP)
  • Albumin and total protein
  • Bilirubin
  • L-lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)
  • Prothrombin time (PT)


RECOMMENDED FOR: 

  • Alcohol abuse
  • Drug abuse
  • Infection 
  • Screen for liver infections, such as hepatitis
  • Monitor the progression of a disease, such as viral or alcoholic hepatitis, and determine how well a treatment is working
  • Measure the severity of a disease, particularly scarring of the liver (cirrhosis)
  • Monitor possible side effects of medications


I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 

  • weakness
  • fatigue or loss of energy
  • weight loss
  • jaundice (yellow skin and eyes)
  • fluid collection in the abdomen, known as ascites
  • discolored bodily discharge (dark urine or light stools)
  • nausea
  • vomiting
  • diarrhea
  • abdominal pain
  • atypical bruising or bleeding


WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?

  • Fasting -avoid all eating and drinking for 8 hours before the test
  • avoid drinking alcohol 
  • avoid taking medications - Statins, Acetaminophen, NSAID
  • Avoiding certain supplements -aloe vera, black cohosh, cascara, chaparral, comfrey, ephedra, or kava


RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children.

HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

Increased ALT, AST, GGT:

  • inflammation or damage to cells in the liver
  • Over-the-counter pain medications ( acetaminophen )
  • Certain prescription medications, including statin drugs used to control cholesterol.
  • Drinking alcohol
  • Heart failure
  • Hepatitis A, B, C 
  • Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease
  • Blocked bile duct
  • Bone disease


LOWER VALUE MEANING 

  • Low level of Albumin might mean damage to liver and lower immune system

TIP

  • Be sure to talk to your provider about what you are currently taking, including over-the-counter medicines, vitamins and supplements
  • liver function tests can also monitor the progression or treatment of a disease and test for the side effects of certain medications.
  • coffee can interfere with blood test results because it contains caffeine and soluble plant matter, is also a diuretic, which means that it will increase how much you pee
  • If you have questions or concerns about test preparations always talk to your health care provider

Urine tests

A urinalysis (also known as a urine test) is a test that examines the visual, chemical and microscopic aspects of your urine 

BASIC 

Color and appearance


Chemical findings


  • Acidity (pH)
  • Protein
  • Glucose (sugar) is usually a sign of diabetes.
  • White blood cells (pus cells)
  • Bilirubin 
  • Blood
  • Ketones


Microscopic findings


  • Bacteria, parasites, or yeast
  • Urinary crystals


EXPERT 

  • Urine creatinine, creatinine clearance
  • Albumin/creatinine ratio
  • Urine osmolality
  • Urine specific gravity
  • Urine electrolyte levels
  • Urine anion gap
  • Hormones
  • Urine pregnancy test 
  • Urine cortisol
  • Urine metanephrines 


RECOMMENDED FOR: 

  • Urinary tract infections 
  • Kidney diseases
  • Kidney stones 
  • Diabetes
  • Liver disease
  • Sexually transmitted diseases 
  • Drug screening
  • Pregnancy 

I NEED THIS TEST IF I HAVE: 

  • Burning during urination
  • Fever
  • Abdominal pain
  • Blood in urine
  • Nausea
  • Vomiting
  • Frequent urination
  • Kidney pain
  • A lot of stress

WHAT SHOULD I DO BEFORE THE TEST?

  • first urination of the morning or midstream 
  • Clean well genital area 

RESULTS 

Normal results vary from laboratory to laboratory and might be slightly different for women and children

HIGHER VALUE MEANING 

White blood cells higher - kidney infection or inflammation 

Red blood cells higher - kidney disease, bladder cancer, blood disorder 

LOWER VALUE MEANING 

Calcium lower - hypoparathyroidism, Hypothyroidism, malabsorption, insufficient Vit D

CT SCAN 

PHOTO DOCTOR   NAME AND SPECIALTY, WHERE HE WORKS - LINK TO FULL BIO. 

WHAT IS A CT SCAN?

CT scan or Computed Tomography scan is an X-ray image made using a form of tomography in which a computer controls the motion of the X-ray source and detectors, processes the data, and produces the image.


RECCOMENDED FOR: 

  • Diagnosis of muscle and bone disorders, such as bone tumors and fractures
  • Chronic back pain
  • Find a tumor, infection or blood clot.
  • Guide procedures such as surgery, biopsy and radiation therapy
  • Monitor cancer, heart disease, lung nodules and liver masses


HOW TO PREPARE FOR A CT SCAN 

  • Refrain from eating or drinking for a few hours before your scan
  • Tell your doctor if you had any allergic reactions
  • Take off some or all of your clothing and wear a hospital gown
  • Remove metal objects, such as a belt, jewelry, dentures and eyeglasses, which might interfere with image results


RISKS 

  • the contrast material can cause medical problems or allergic reactions.
  • most reactions are mild and result in a rash or itchiness. In rare instances, an allergic reaction can be serious, even life-threatening. 
  • Pregnancy , breastfeeding


AFTER CT SCAN 

  • After the exam you can return to your normal routine. 
  • If you were given contrast material, drink lots of fluids to help your kidneys remove the contrast material from your body


  • In some cases, you may be asked to wait for a short time before leaving to ensure that you feel well after the exam.

RESULTS

  • CT images are stored as electronic data files and are usually reviewed on a computer screen. A radiologist interprets these images and sends a report to your doctor

TIP

  • If you are very stressed before the test you can use a natural relaxation medication or even an anxiolytic
